narrative: Fire and climate themes dominate this week's haul. Two studies dig into fire adaptation and management: Pinus yunnanensis shows stronger serotiny in more fire-prone areas, while prescribed burning in sub-Mediterranean pine forests had only minor effects on pine processionary moth survival, suggesting fuel-reduction burns can proceed without unintended pest consequences. On climate, a two-decade oak-mouse dataset shows the timing of warming during tree reproduction — not just its magnitude — determines whether mast-driven food webs boom or bust, and a 35-year elk-cattle dataset finds drought intensifies competitive avoidance between wild and domestic herbivores rather than the facilitation predicted by grazing-optimisation theory. In movement and methods, new step-selection and three-dimensional correlated-random-walk frameworks extend habitat-selection modelling to flying and swimming animals, and a wingbeat-frequency method offers a non-invasive way to track mass gain during foraging. Conservation Biology carries two standout syntheses: three decades of IUCN Red List assessments reveal the extinction-risk trajectory of sharks, rays and chimaeras, and a five-year retrospective on the NSW Saving our Species program tests how well expert-elicited predictions matched real management outcomes. On evolution, a review argues climate adaptation practice has much to learn from evolutionary theory about when adaptation helps versus when it produces maladaptation.

methods_radar.title: Warming increases trophic cascade strength in an aquatic food chain
methods_radar.note: Uses Bayesian dynamical modelling to quantify trophic cascade strength under warming.

methods_radar.title: Effective fine‐scale drone monitoring of wild Asian elephants for conflict mitigation and ecological research
methods_radar.note: Applies drone monitoring to track wild Asian elephants for conflict mitigation.

methods_radar.title: Resolving Emergent Patterns in Community Genetics With Environmental DNA
methods_radar.note: Environmental DNA applied to resolve community genetics patterns across space and time.
